assignment_description:
How Geology Works
⍞ Materials: The Field Guide to Geology

➜ NOTE
If you have a rock and/or fossil kit, you may find it useful to keep it nearby throughout this course. As different materials are mentioned in your various books, you can reference these materials in your kit(s).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Read about 12.5 pages per week. Narrate in a way that is helpful to you. Copy any diagrams or images that are helpful.

• PLAN WEEKLY
⬚ nature walk - record observations
⬚ science free read

Practical Interpretation
⍞ Materials: Roadside Geology & Notebook

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Divide up your state or regional geology book so that you finish it this term. (If you are not able to finish it in one term, that is okay. You might choose to continue it next term or to leave off.) As you read, map your state or region, showing the interesting geological features. Plan field trips, if possible, to some of the sites. Record your observations and findings.

Identification

➜ INTRO
This is your day each week to prepare for your field studies using a mixture of resources provided for help, time for planning, and to spend time with the rocks you have found.

➜ RESEARCH
Start at Gator Girl Rocks to begin your journey. The How to Find Rocks website is helpful, as well. You will want to read about the laws that pertain to rockhounding and visit the links under your state. Read about any local resources, and begin making a plan. You might want to visit local nature centers and museums, and ask about local geology guides or guided tours. You might want to track down any local clubs or groups to join. Ask local experts to see their collections. Ask them for help and direction in finding and identifying what is readily available locally. Ask for recommendations for identification guides that are appropriate for your area and that they have found helpful.
∞ Link: Gator Girl Rocks
∞ Link: How to Find Rocks
∞ Link: Rock Clubs by State

➜ PREP FOR FIELD DAY
• Decide where you will explore on your field day.
• Check the weather and confirm when you will go.
• Collect any tools or materials that you might want to take with you.

Geology Literature
⍞ Materials: Annals of the Former World

➜ INTRO
People say this book is the "Literature of Geology". See what you think by the end of the term. McPhee travels across the country with various geologists, writing about the geological wonders along the way. He is writing for people who are not geologists, but that doesn't mean that the text is easy. Take in what you can and enjoy the views out the window.

This is a big book. Read Book 1 (Basin and Range), and then decide if you want to keep reading in order, read about places near you, or read about ones that you have never visited. Go at your own pace. If you read and narrate around 20 pages per week, you will get a good way through the book by the end of Term 2. Then you can decide if you want to finish as a free read or not.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Be sure to look at the map on p.2-3 and read the Narrative Table of Contents before plunging into Chapter 1. Refer to the maps and charts in the text and at the end of the book while you are reading. You may want to look up unfamiliar terms as you read and make a collection of geological terms. Come up with an interesting way to narrate, perhaps an illustrated map or travel journal?
~ 20 pages

Flex Day/Current Events
⍞ Materials: Selected geology literature, lab/field supplies, or internet access

➜ INTRO
Use this flex day to finish the week's reading from any of your geology books (scheduled or by your own selection), continue any lab/field activities, or read some geology current events (linked in the lesson and in your Quick Links). In this way, you may use this day to tailor the course to be more literary by reading an additional text, such as The Map that Changed the World, or more active by pursuing a special study of interest.
∞ Article Link: Geology Current Events

Identification

➜ RESEARCH
Google your state's Geological Survey to see if they have any online resources that would be helpful to you, or check out the USGS maps online. If an office is nearby, they may have some maps or other resources you could pick up. Also, see if they will answer any of your questions about your local geology. If not, see if they know someone you could talk to.

Present What You've Learned

➜ PREPARE
For one of your exam questions next week, you will be asked to give an informal presentation to your teacher on what you have discovered so far about your local geology and geological history. Use this time to gather your thoughts and to think through how you want to do that. You can be as creative as you would like. You will do a more formal presentation at the end of the year. This is more like an update on what you have discovered so far.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geology Lessons — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Field Guide to Geology: Explain in what ways the earth's crust changes.

· Field Guide to Geology: Describe different ways that rocks are formed through the changes in the earth's crust.

· Explain how you identified a found rock this term. OR Tell about a geology current event.

· Annals of a Former World: Explain some aspect of geology that McPhee has brought to life for you.

· Explain what you have discovered so far about your local geology and geological history. Share your notebook.

Practical Interpretation
⍞ Materials: 101 American Geo-Sites You've Gotta See & Notebook

➜ INTRO
You read about your state or region last term; this term, we will expand the survey to the country's borders.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
~ 2-5 sites per week, depending on how much time you want to spend with each one. You might research the type(s) of rocks found at the site and/or examine the geological maps of the site. As you read, sketch a map of the US that shows the location of the sites and what is there.

Identification

➜ RESEARCH
Where do you plan to visit this term? Is there a specific rock type or formation that you want to learn more about? Use the resources that you discovered last term to make a plan for this term. What questions about your local geology do you have? How will you find the answers?

Present What You've Learned

➜ PREPARE
For one of your exam questions next week, you will be asked to give another informal presentation to your teacher on what you have discovered during this second term about your local geology and geological history. Use this time to gather your thoughts and think through how you want to do that. You can be as creative as you would like. You will do a more formal presentation at the end of the year. This is more like an update on what you have discovered so far.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geology Lessons — TERM 2 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Field Guide to Geology: Describe how water shapes the land through rivers OR seas OR ice.

· Field Guide to Geology: Explain how relative dating works.

· Explain how you identified a found rock this term. OR Tell about a geology current event.

· Annals of a Former World: Explain some aspect of geology that McPhee has brought to life for you.

· Tell what you have discovered this term about your local geology and geological history. Share your notebook.

Present What You've Learned

➜PLAN
Make a plan for how you will take all the information you have researched on your local area: fieldwork, experiments, photos, illustrations, drawings, maps, and specimens, and prepare a presentation about your local geology and its history, along with any other places you visited.

You might type up notes and give your presentation as a talk; you might want to create a PowerPoint or Google Slides presentation. Or, you might choose to design something in Canva or another visual tool. You might like to create a display that uses maps and charts with your specimens laid out and labeled. You might want to design an infographic that uses your specimens to describe your local area. You decide. Be as creative as you would like. You can include poetry or music that you write, poetry and music that you find, and/or incorporate paintings, drawings, or illustrations.

You will have three weeks to work on it. Then, you will give your presentation to a live audience during exam week.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geology Lessons — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Tell the story of one of the rocks from The Story of the Earth in 25 Rocks.

· The Story of the Earth in 25 Rocks: Explain how a geologist is like a detective.

· Explain how you identified a found rock this term. OR Tell about a geology current event.

· Tell about a geological site that you read about that you would like to visit someday. Where is it? What is interesting about it? What would you hope to see or learn?

· Give a presentation on your local geology and geological history. Share your notebook.
